Felipe, you have twenty minutes and a nervous board. Where are you taking us?
Let me start with the agenda: the headline number, one caveat, and the fix. The bottom line is that we are two million below plan.
Two million. Is that the revenue line or the margin?
That is exactly the right question. Let me circle back to it in ninety seconds, when the slide in front of it makes the answer obvious.
Fine. But do not gloss over the margin. The last CFO did, and it cost him this room.
I will not. Let me drill down into it now, because I want to flag one figure with a proper caveat: the gross margin fell two points, and here is precisely why.
Good. And the number you owe me, revenue or margin?

Revenue. In ballpark terms the shortfall is one point four million of revenue and six hundred thousand of margin; I will confirm the exact split tonight. To sum up: below plan, one honest caveat, and a fix that is already working.
Let me start with the agenda and the headline number.
The bottom line is that we are two million below plan.
That is exactly the right question. Let me circle back to it in two minutes.
I want to flag one figure here that deserves a proper caveat.
To sum up: the headline number, the one caveat, and the fix.

Let me tell you the difference between a CFO who presents and a CFO who holds the room, because it is not the numbers. Both of them have the same numbers. The difference is that one of them tells you where you are at every moment, and the other one makes you guess. Here is what I do now, and I did not do it for the first ten years. In the first thirty seconds I give the agenda, out loud, and I give the headline number, out loud, before anything else. I do not build up to it. I do not save it for the end like a magician. I say, the bottom line is this, we are two million below plan, here is why, and here is the fix, and I will drill down into each of those in turn. And then, and this is the part people miss, when a hard question comes in the middle, I do not panic and answer it badly. I say, that is exactly the right question, let me circle back to it in two minutes when the number in front of it will make it easier to answer. And they let me, every time, because I told them where we were going. Control of a room is not confidence. It is signposting.

I coach executives on presentations, and I am going to tell you the single most common way that brilliant finance people lose a board. They gloss over the one number they are frightened of. They speed up, they lower their voice, they bury it in the middle of a slide full of other figures, and they hope. And every experienced director in that room can smell it, because the energy changes for exactly four seconds. So here is the rule I give them. Name the caveat before anyone asks for it. Slow down on the weak number, do not speed up. Say, I want to flag one figure here that deserves a proper caveat, and then give it, cleanly, with the reason. Two things happen. First, you look like the most senior person in the room, because only the senior ones volunteer the bad news. And second, you take the weapon out of the hands of the person who was about to raise it. A presentation is not a performance of confidence. It is a map you hand to the room, and the best maps mark the swamp, they do not hide it.
